template: Match each term to its definition in a food chain.

pairs.right: Makes its own food by photosynthesis

explanation: Producers (plants) make food. Primary consumers (herbivores) eat producers. Secondary consumers eat primary consumers. Decomposers (bacteria/fungi) break down dead material recycling nutrients.

template: A producer has a biomass of {{bm}} kg. Only {{pct}}% of biomass is transferred to the next trophic level. Calculate the biomass of the primary consumer.
explanation: Biomass transferred = {{bm}} × {{pct}}/100 = {{ans}} kg. Typically only 10% of biomass is transferred between trophic levels — the rest is lost as heat (respiration), waste, or indigestible material.

template: Explain why food chains rarely have more than four or five trophic levels.

answerConfig.textAnswer: only about 10% of biomass is transferred at each level so there is not enough energy to support further levels
acceptedAnswers: energy is lost at each trophic level so insufficient energy remains for more levels
most energy is lost as heat/waste at each step leaving too little for additional levels

explanation: At each trophic level, only ~10% of energy/biomass is transferred to the next level — the rest is lost as heat (respiration), waste, and indigestible material. After 4–5 levels, so little energy remains that it cannot support a viable population.

template: Match each process to its role in the carbon cycle.

pairs.right: Returns carbon from dead organisms to soil/atmosphere

explanation: Carbon cycle: photosynthesis removes CO₂ → stored in organic molecules → respiration/combustion release CO₂ back → decomposers break down dead matter returning carbon to the atmosphere and soil.

template: Explain the role of bacteria in the nitrogen cycle.

answerConfig.markScheme: 1. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert atmospheric N₂ to ammonia/nitrates in soil (1) / 2. Nitrifying bacteria convert ammonia to nitrites then nitrates (1) / 3. Plants absorb nitrates through roots to make proteins (1) / 4. Decomposers break down dead organisms releasing ammonia (ammonification) (1) / 5. Denitrifying bacteria convert nitrates back to N₂ gas (1) — max 4

explanation: Bacteria play several roles: nitrogen-fixing bacteria convert N₂ → ammonia (free-living or in root nodules), nitrifying bacteria convert ammonia → nitrites → nitrates (usable by plants), decomposers release ammonia from dead matter, denitrifying bacteria return N₂ to atmosphere.

template: State THREE reasons why biodiversity is important.

options: Source of new medicines from plants/organisms
correctOptions: Source of new medicines from plants/organisms

explanation: Biodiversity is important for: ecosystem stability (complex food webs are more resilient), discovery of new medicines (e.g. antibiotics from fungi), and maintaining genetic resources for agriculture and biotechnology.

template: Select ALL possible effects of global warming on living organisms.
explanation: Global warming effects: species shift their ranges towards poles/higher altitudes as temperatures rise, migration and breeding times change, species unable to adapt face extinction. Overall biodiversity tends to decrease, not increase.

template: Explain TWO ways deforestation affects the carbon cycle.

answerConfig.textAnswer: fewer trees means less CO2 removed by photosynthesis and burning trees releases carbon stored in wood
acceptedAnswers: less photosynthesis reducing CO2 absorption and burning releases stored CO2
reduces CO2 uptake and increases CO2 release through burning

explanation: Deforestation affects the carbon cycle in two main ways: (1) fewer trees means less CO₂ removed from the atmosphere by photosynthesis; (2) burning trees releases the carbon stored in wood as CO₂, increasing atmospheric CO₂ and contributing to global warming.

template: A quadrat of area {{qa}} m² contains {{count}} daisy plants. The total study area is {{total}} m². Estimate the total number of daisy plants.
explanation: Population estimate = (count in quadrat / quadrat area) × total area = ({{count}} / {{qa}}) × {{total}} = {{ans}} plants.

template: Select ALL conditions that speed up decomposition.
explanation: Decomposition is fastest in warm, moist, aerobic conditions — these are optimal for the enzymes and metabolic activity of decomposing bacteria and fungi. Cold, dry, or anaerobic conditions slow decomposition significantly.

template: Order the stages of the water cycle.

items: Water evaporates from oceans and lakes
Precipitation falls as rain or snow
Water returns to oceans via rivers

explanation: Water cycle: evaporation (oceans/lakes/transpiration) → water vapour rises and cools → condensation (clouds form) → precipitation (rain/snow) → runoff/rivers → back to oceans.

template: What is an indicator species? Give an example.

answerConfig.textAnswer: a species whose presence or absence indicates the environmental quality for example lichens indicate clean air
acceptedAnswers: organism used to assess pollution levels — e.g. freshwater invertebrates indicate water quality
species that shows the condition of an environment — e.g. lichens (air), mayfly larvae (clean water)

explanation: Indicator species are organisms sensitive to environmental conditions. Their presence or absence shows the quality of the environment. Lichens indicate clean air (sensitive to SO₂). Mayfly larvae indicate clean water. Rat-tailed maggots indicate polluted water.

template: Select ALL greenhouse gases that contribute to global warming.
explanation: The main greenhouse gases are CO₂ (from burning fossil fuels), methane (from cattle, landfill, rice paddies), and water vapour. Oxygen and nitrogen are major atmospheric gases but are not greenhouse gases.

template: How is acid rain formed and what effects does it have?

answerConfig.textAnswer: sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides from burning fossil fuels dissolve in rainwater forming acids that damage plants soil and aquatic life
acceptedAnswers: SO2 and NO from combustion dissolve in rain creating acidic conditions that harm ecosystems

explanation: Burning fossil fuels releases SO₂ and NOₓ. These dissolve in atmospheric water to form sulphuric and nitric acid — acid rain. Effects: damages leaves, leaches nutrients from soil, acidifies lakes and rivers killing aquatic life.

template: Suggest TWO benefits of rewilding a habitat.

options: Reintroducing apex predators restores trophic balance
Provides ecosystem services such as flood prevention
correctOptions: Reintroducing apex predators restores trophic balance

explanation: Rewilding — restoring ecosystems to a natural state — can restore habitats, increase biodiversity, reintroduce species that maintain ecosystem balance (e.g. wolves controlling deer populations), and provide ecosystem services like carbon storage and flood management.

template: State THREE reasons why energy is lost between trophic levels.

options: Energy lost as heat through respiration
Some material not digested — lost in faeces
Energy stored in bones and hair is unavailable
correctOptions: Energy lost as heat through respiration
Some material not digested — lost in faeces

explanation: Energy is lost between trophic levels via: (1) heat from respiration — only ~10% of energy in food is converted to new biomass; (2) uneaten material; (3) indigestible material in faeces; (4) excretion of waste products.

template: Why is a transect used rather than random quadrats when studying a gradient such as a rocky shore?

answerConfig.textAnswer: a transect allows systematic sampling along an environmental gradient to show how species distribution changes with distance
acceptedAnswers: shows change in species along a gradient rather than random distribution
records how organisms change systematically along a continuous environmental change

explanation: A belt or line transect is used when there is a systematic change in the environment (gradient). Quadrats are placed at regular intervals along the transect, allowing ecologists to see how species distribution changes continuously along the gradient.

template: Select ALL factors that could cause a population to decrease in size.
explanation: Population decreases with: more predators (increased death rate), disease (kills individuals), habitat loss (reduced carrying capacity). Increased food availability and reduced competition would increase population size.

template: Explain what is meant by interdependence in an ecosystem, using an example.

answerConfig.textAnswer: organisms in an ecosystem depend on each other for example predators depend on prey for food and plants depend on insects for pollination
acceptedAnswers: species rely on each other — removing one affects others e.g. bees pollinating flowers
organisms are connected through feeding relationships and other interactions

explanation: Interdependence means species in an ecosystem rely on each other for survival. For example: plants need insects for pollination; predators depend on prey; prey control plant populations. Removing any species can have cascading effects throughout the ecosystem.

template: Explain how fertiliser runoff causes eutrophication and fish to die.

answerConfig.markScheme: 1. Nitrates/phosphates run into water causing algal bloom (1) / 2. Algae blocks light reaching aquatic plants (1) / 3. Plants die — decomposers increase in number (1) / 4. Decomposers use oxygen for respiration — oxygen levels fall (1) / 5. Fish and aerobic organisms die due to lack of oxygen (1) — max 4

explanation: Fertiliser nitrates/phosphates enter water → algal bloom → algae block light → aquatic plants die → decomposers multiply and respire aerobically → oxygen depleted → fish and aerobic organisms die (deoxygenation).

template: Match each conservation method to its description.

pairs.right: Stores seeds of plants for future use
Breeds endangered species in zoos for reintroduction
Protected area where fishing is banned
Planting trees to replace those that were cut down

explanation: Conservation methods: seed banks preserve plant genetic diversity, captive breeding programmes help endangered species recover, marine reserves allow fish populations to recover, reforestation restores habitats.

template: True or false: A pyramid of biomass always has the largest bar at the bottom.
explanation: True. In a pyramid of biomass, producers (bottom) always have the greatest biomass. Each successive trophic level has less biomass because energy is lost at each stage — the pyramid narrows upward.

template: Evaluate the impact of human activities on biodiversity and suggest how these impacts can be reduced.

answerConfig.markScheme: Negative impacts (max 3): deforestation destroys habitats reducing species (1) / pollution damages ecosystems (1) / overfishing reduces fish populations (1) / climate change alters habitats (1) / introduction of invasive species (1). Reducing impacts (max 3): protected areas/nature reserves (1) / sustainable farming/fishing (1) / reducing carbon emissions (1) / reforestation/rewilding (1) / international agreements e.g. CITES (1). Conclusion required for full marks (1).

explanation: Human activities reduce biodiversity through habitat destruction, pollution, overexploitation, and climate change. Impacts can be reduced through conservation, sustainable resource use, pollution control, and international cooperation.

template: Explain why a complex food web is more stable than a simple food chain.

answerConfig.textAnswer: in a complex food web if one species is lost others can feed on alternative prey so the ecosystem can continue functioning
acceptedAnswers: more feeding relationships mean loss of one species has less impact
alternative food sources mean predators are not entirely dependent on one prey species

explanation: A complex food web has many interconnections. If one species declines, predators can switch to alternative prey and the ecosystem continues to function. In a simple food chain, loss of one species can collapse the entire chain.
